cards.back: A function that MUST be evaluated at compile-time. Unlike constexpr functions which can evaluate at either compile-time or runtime, consteval functions can only evaluate at compile-time.

cards.text: To create a function that must evaluate at compile-time, use {{c1::consteval}} instead of {{c2::constexpr}}.

cards.front: What is the key difference between constexpr and consteval functions?
cards.back:
• constexpr: CAN evaluate at compile-time, MAY evaluate at runtime
  • consteval: MUST evaluate at compile-time, NEVER at runtime

cards.question: Why does this code fail to compile?
cards.answer: square() is a consteval function and must be evaluated at compile-time, but num is not a constant expression, so the function cannot be evaluated.
cards.explanation: consteval functions require all arguments to be constant expressions. To fix this, either make num constexpr or change square() to constexpr.

cards.concept: When to use consteval instead of constexpr
cards.explanation:
Use consteval when:
  • The function MUST execute at compile-time
  • You want to guarantee no runtime overhead
  • You want to catch errors if non-constant arguments are passed
  • The function is a helper for compile-time computation
consteval provides stronger compile-time guarantees than constexpr.
examples: consteval int getMagicNumber() { return 42; }  // Always compile-time
constexpr int square(int x) { return x*x; }    // Flexible (compile or runtime)

cards.front: What happens if you try to call a consteval function with runtime arguments?
cards.back: The code will fail to compile. consteval functions require all arguments to be constant expressions known at compile-time.

cards.text: consteval functions are also called {{c1::immediate functions}} because they must produce an {{c2::immediate (compile-time) result}}.

cards.front: In which C++ standard was consteval introduced?
cards.back: C++20. consteval is a C++20 feature.

cards.question: Is this code valid?
cards.explanation: getValue() is consteval and returns a constant expression (100). It can be used to initialize a constexpr variable. The function will be evaluated at compile-time.

cards.front: What performance benefit does consteval provide over constexpr?
cards.back: consteval guarantees zero runtime overhead because the function is ALWAYS evaluated at compile-time. constexpr functions might evaluate at runtime, potentially adding overhead.

cards.text: {{c1::consteval}} functions are also called {{c2::immediate functions}} and were introduced in {{c3::C++20}}.
Use {{c1::consteval}} when a function MUST execute at compile-time, and {{c2::constexpr}} when it CAN execute at compile-time but MAY execute at runtime.
